data.title: AI data centers are forcing dirty ‚Äòpeaker‚Äô power plants back into service
data.description: CHICAGO, Dec. 23 - In Chicago‚Äôs working-class Pilsen neighborhood, a 60s-era oil-fired power ‚Äåplant rises up from an industrial lot behind Dvorak Park, which in warmer weather is packed with children climbing on its colorful playground and zooming down slides. The rarely-used eight-unit Fisk power plant owned by Houston-based NRG Energy was scheduled to retire next year. But then came electricity demands from artificial intelligence.¬Ý Prices shot up in the country‚Äôs biggest power market ‚Äì PJM Interconnection ‚Äì as electricity requests from data centers exceeded existing supplies, sounding the alarm over power shortfalls, and making Fisk and other plants like it suddenly profitable. "We believe there's an economic case to keep them around, so we withdrew the retirement notice,‚Äù said Matt Pistner, senior vice president of generation at NRG, of Fisk‚Äôs eight power-generating units.¬Ý¬Ý The Fisk power plant is among a growing number of so-called ‚Äúpeaker‚Äù electric generating units being pressed into duty across the U.S. as the nation‚Äôs electrical grids struggle to keep up with growing demand from data centers powering Big Tech‚Äôs investments in artificial intelligence. Peakers, which are meant to run only in short bursts during periods of spiking electricity demand, help stave off blackouts by supplying power on a moment‚Äôs notice. But there‚Äôs a trade-off: these often decades-old, fossil-fueled facilities emit more pollution when they are running and cost more to produce electricity than continuous power plants. A Reuters analysis of filings with the country‚Äôs biggest power grid shows that about 60 per cent of oil, gas and coal power plants slated for retirement in PJM postponed or cancelled those plans this year. Most of the plants averting shutdowns are peaker units.¬Ý The Fisk peakers were built on the site of a now-defunct coal-fired electricity generating station that operated for over a century. After years of fierce opposition by local residents, the coal plant shut more than a decade ago, but eight peaking units that run on petroleum oil continue to operate on the site. ‚ÄúWhen we found out that the coal plant was closing but there was still going to be power produced at the site, it was very disappointing,‚Äù said Jerry ‚ÄåMead-Lucero, a longtime advocate for the closure of the Fisk coal station who spent most of his adult life in Pilsen. Following the coal plant closure, pollution plummeted, but it didn‚Äôt vanish. Sulfur dioxide ranged from about 2 to as much as 25 tons per year from the site, ‚Äåaccording to the Environmental Protection Agency, as the eight-unit peaker plant occasionally lumbered to life to feed the grid. "That's not an insignificant amount considering the low chimneys and homes nearby," said Brian Urbaszewski, Director of Environmental Health Programs for Respiratory Health Association, an Illinois nonprofit that focuses on helping people with respiratory disease.¬Ý¬Ý¬Ý¬Ý DIRTY POWER Because they were built for speed instead of efficiency, peakers often do not have pollution controls like mercury scrubbers, which remove the toxic chemical from the power plants‚Äô emissions, and filters for particulate matter, according to academic and federal government research.¬Ý Some also have lower smokestacks, or chimneys, environmental advocates say,¬Ý meaning pollution can be more concentrated locally. Keeping peakers running longer may accelerate under U.S. President Donald Trump‚Äôs administration, which said it was exploring ways to tap into existing power sources, including peaker plants and other emergency systems, to quickly meet the massive new electricity demand. ‚ÄúThere are a ton of peaker plants that could operate more,‚Äù U.S. Energy Secretary Chris Wright told Reuters in an interview in September, adding that clean air regulations have kept more from running more frequently. ‚ÄúThe biggest targets are spare capacity on the grid today.‚Äù While peaker plants contribute about 3 per cent of the country‚Äôs power, they have the total capacity to produce 19 per cent, according to a report by the U.S. Government Accountability Office. Tapping into that spare capacity, however, could mean more harmful emissions being spewed into neighborhoods that are often already overburdened with environmental hazards. The country's roughly 1,000 peaker plants are disproportionately located in low-income communities of color, according to academic and federal government research, meaning that extending the plants‚Äô lives could leave vulnerable Americans to bear the brunt of more pollution. A 2022 study of formerly ‚Äúredlined‚Äù U.S. communities, which were cut off from financial services like mortgages for being predominantly Black or immigrant, found that residents were 53 per cent more ‚Äçlikely to have had a peaker plant built nearby since the year 2000 than in non-redlined areas. ‚ÄúIf you were a redlined neighborhood, you were more likely to have a fossil fuel power plant built nearby, and we saw that relationship was even stronger for peaker plants,‚Äù said UCLA professor of environmental health sciences Lara Cushing, who led the study.¬Ý POWER DEMANDS STRAINING THE GRID Most of the country's peaker plants were built during two periods of growth in energy consumption: in the mid-20th century as electrical appliances became common household items, and at the turn of the millennium as the economy grew and computers gained popularity. Afterwards, as energy-sapping devices and infrastructure became more efficient, U.S. power demand waned and many fossil-fired power plants shut. Meanwhile, solar and wind farms, which only produce power when the sun is shining and the wind is blowing, began to supply more of the country‚Äôs energy. "We're kind of making the old system work harder and that's part of why we're seeing this increased use of plants operating as peakers," said Frank Rusco, a director with the Government Accountability Office, which was directed by U.S. Congress, at the urging of environmental justice groups, to study the use of peaker plants and how they intersect with American communities.¬Ý The study found that natural gas peaker plants emit 1.6 times more sulfur dioxide for each unit of electricity produced on a median basis compared to non-peaker¬Ýplants. Fisk is ‚Äãpart of the nation‚Äôs largest electrical grid, PJM Interconnection, which stretches across 13 states and covers the world‚Äôs biggest concentration of data centers. Demand from AI ‚Äådata centers is threatening to engulf the grid‚Äôs power reserves, and it is already driving up prices.¬Ý¬Ý Prices paid to power suppliers in PJM to ensure plants run at times of spiking demand soared by more than 800 per cent this summer, compared to a year earlier. That made owning peaker power plants much more lucrative. ‚ÄúIt is clear today, nationally, that electricity demand is outstripping supply ‚Äì the market reflects this, and generators are responding,‚Äù PJM spokesman Jeff Shields said. ‚ÄúWe cannot afford to lose existing generation while we continue to bring on new generation to keep pace with the electricity needs of data centers and other large loads powering the country‚Äôs economy.‚Äù About 23 oil, gas and coal power plants in PJM territory were scheduled to retire starting in 2025 or shortly after, according ‚Äãto a Reuters analysis of letters sent to PJM Interconnection by power companies. Since January, U.S. power companies, the grid operator, and the federal government have delayed or cancelled the retirements of 13 of those power plants, the letters showed. Of those plants that averted closure, 11 were peakers. Among those delayed were the roughly 55-year-old units at the ‚ÄúEddystone‚Äù plant outside of Philadelphia, owned by Constellation Energy, which were ordered to keep running by the Department of Energy. The Wagner ‚ÅÝpeaker near Baltimore, meanwhile, was kept alive at the request of PJM while the grid operator coordinates on the transmission needed for the removal of the generator. Many of the retained power plants ‚Äåwere built as peakers, while others were initially intended to be around-the-clock power, but later downgraded to run only during emergencies. ‚ÄòLAST LINE OF DEFENSE‚Äô Fisk owner NRG Energy says peakers are essential safeguards for the grid that are being called on more often not just for data centers but for the electrification of manufacturing and transportation, and to avert blackouts caused by increasingly severe winter storms and summer heatwaves. Having the Fisk peakers in the city ‚Äãmeans that Chicago doesn‚Äôt need to import electricity in the case of an emergency when outside power sources go down. ‚ÄúThey really are the last line of defense, and the shock absorber, for the system,‚Äù said Matt Pistner of NRG Energy. ‚ÄúWhen they‚Äôre needed, there is no other place to go.‚Äù While NRG has owned power generating sources from nuclear energy to wind and solar, oil-fired peakers add another layer of certainty by ensuring the power fuel source can be stored on site, Pistner said. ‚ÄúDuring its run times, the power plant consistently operates within federal and state environmental regulations ‚Äî and we are proud of its record," an NRG spokesman told Reuters separately.¬Ý Energy experts say there are alternatives to peakers. Investing in more robust transmission lines could ‚Äçtransport electricity from parts of the country with oversupplies of power to those with shortfalls.¬Ý "If we do that, the system would run more efficiently and you would probably have a reduction in the amount of reliance on peakers," said the GAO's Rusco. Batteries, which are undergoing technological improvements to store power for longer, could also replace many peaker units, according to clean energy advocates. In the meantime, as AI power demand rises, communities like Pilsen, which have successfully ‚ÅÝfought to close some pollution sources in recent history, may find peaker plants more difficult to fight. ‚ÄúIt all adds up to significant cost increases for electricity consumers and significant increases in local pollution and wi

data.title: Fleet management firm Motive Technologies reveals revenue growth in US IPO paperwork
data.description: Dec 23 : ‚ÄåAI-powered fleet management company Motive Technologies on Tuesday disclosed a rise in nine-month revenue in its U.S. initial public offering filing, as companies line up to list early next year. The San ‚ÄåFrancisco, California-based company reported a net ‚Äåloss of $138.5 million on $327.3 million revenue for the nine-month period ended September 30, compared with a loss of $113.9 million on $268.9 million revenue in the same period a year earlier. The ‚ÄçU.S. IPO market has regained momentum in 2025 but expectations of a stronger rebound were curbed by tariff-driven volatility, a prolonged government ‚Äãshutdown and a ‚Äålate-year selloff in AI stocks. Motive Technologies will list on the NYSE under the "MTVE" ‚Äãsymbol. Motive is a software firm providing AI-powered tools ‚ÅÝto manage fleets and ‚Äåphysical operations, helping customers improve safety, ‚Äãmanage vehicles and equipment, track spending and automate workforce and compliance tasks across ‚Äçindustries including logistics, construction, energy and manufacturing. J.P. Morgan, ‚ÅÝCitigroup, Jefferies and Barclays are among the ‚Äãunderwriters for the ‚Äåoffering.

data.title: Paramount's new offer for Warner Bros is not sufficient, major investor says
data.description: Dec 23 : Paramount Skydance's latest¬Ýoffer to buy Warner Bros Discovery is still not good enough for prominent shareholder Harris Oakmark, the investor told Reuters on Monday.¬Ý Warner Bros' fifth largest shareholder, owning 96 million shares or about¬Ý4 per cent of shares as of the end of September, said it would hold out for more from the Ellison family-controlled Paramount. "The changes in Paramount‚Äôs new offer were necessary, but not sufficient," Harris Oakmark portfolio manager and Director of U.S. Research Alex Fitch said in an email to Reuters. "We see the two deals as a toss-up, and there is a cost to changing paths. If Paramount is serious ‚Äåabout winning, they‚Äôre going to need to provide a greater incentive." Paramount on Monday amended its $108.4 billion ‚Äåhostile bid for the storied Hollywood studio to bolster its financing. Oracle co-founder Larry Ellison, whose son David owns Paramount, is now personally guaranteeing $40.4 billion of the bid to secure Warner Bros, which owns HBO Max and controls the Harry Potter, Lord of the Rings and Superman franchises. Questions about the financing, much of which was held in a revocable trust, had some Warner Bros investors unsure whether they would accept the offer. Paramount also increased the fee it will pay to $5.8 billion from $5 billion if regulators don't approve the deal, to match a competing offer ‚Äçfrom Netflix , although it didn't raise its $30-a-share bid.¬Ý 'TOP SHELF MEDIA ASSETS' Warner Bros investors now have until January 21, extended from January 8, to accept or reject the so-called tender offer. The board of Warner Bros unanimously recommended on Wednesday that shareholders reject Paramount's earlier bid in favor of Netflix's offer, saying the financing didn't provide a "full backstop". Even though Netflix's cash offer of $23.25 a share is lower, the board said its bid was superior because the ‚Äãfinancing was more secure and it includes $4.50 in ‚Äåshares of Netflix common stock as well as whatever Warner Bros can get when it spins out Discovery Global as part of the deal. The bidding war speaks to the quality of Warner Bros assets, said Yussef Gheriani, chief investment officer ‚Äãof Chicago investment firm IHT Wealth Management, which owns 16,000 shares of Warner Bros, 6,500 shares of Netflix and 60,000 of Paramount. "It‚Äôs really rare to ‚ÅÝget an opportunity to add top shelf media assets to ‚Äåyour portfolio," he said, adding that he'll likely follow the board's advice on the sale. "They know the business inside out and have a ‚Äãbetter grasp of the nuances associated with the deal than we do." Investor Thomas Poehling, who owns 484,000 shares of Warner Bros and 639,000 of Paramount, said he'll likely take the revised offer if Netflix doesn't counter because Paramount has ‚Äça better chance of winning approval from regulators.¬Ý Ellison's guarantee "adds a lot of stability to that offer and that removes a lot of the financing ‚ÅÝuncertainty," he said. Gheriani and Poehling aren't the only investors to hold shares in the rival movie studios. Vanguard, State Street and BlackRock are Warner Bros' three largest ‚Äãshareholders, controlling at least 22 per cent of the ‚Äåcompany between them. All three are also among the top ten investors in Paramount and Netflix. None commented for ‚Äçthis ‚Äãarticle.

data.title: Motive Technologies files for US IPO
data.description: Dec ‚Äå23 : AI-powered automated operations platform Motive Technologies on Tuesday filed for an initial public offering ‚Äåin the United ‚ÄåStates. The U.S. IPO market has regained momentum in 2025 but expectations of a ‚Äçstronger rebound were curbed by tariff-driven volatility, a prolonged government ‚Äãshutdown and ‚Äåa late-year selloff in AI stocks. Motive ‚ÄãTechnologies will list on ‚ÅÝthe NYSE ‚Äåunder the symbol "MTVE". J.P. ‚ÄãMorgan, Citigroup and Barclays are among ‚Äçthe underwriters for the ‚ÅÝoffering.

data.title: Japan to test mine rare-earth mud from deep seabed¬Ý
data.description: TOKYO: ‚ÄåJapan will conduct test mining of rare-earth-rich mud from the deep seabed off Minamitori Island, some 1,900km southeast of Tokyo, from Jan 11 to Feb 14, the head of the government-backed project said on Tuesday (Dec 23).¬Ý The operation will mark the world's first attempt to continuously lift rare-earth mud from a depth of around 6,000m ‚Äåon to a vessel. Tokyo, like its Western allies, is ‚Äåseeking to secure stable supplies of critical minerals as China, the dominant supplier of rare earths, tightens export controls.¬Ý "One of our missions is to build a supply chain for domestically produced rare earths to ensure stable supply of minerals essential to industry," Shoichi Ishii, program director of the Cabinet Office's national ‚Äçplatform for innovative ocean developments, told reporters. The Japanese government is pressing ahead with a national project as part of broader efforts to strengthen maritime and economic security. The January test will focus on connecting the deep-sea mining system and confirming its ‚Äãability to lift 350 metric ‚Äåtonnes of rare-earth mud per day. Environmental impacts will be monitored both onboard and on the seabed throughout the operation. No production target ‚Äãhas been set, but if successful, a full-scale mining trial will be conducted in ‚ÅÝFebruary 2027. The government-funded project has spent ‚Äåabout ¬•40 billion (US$256 million) since 2018, Ishii said, though estimated reserves ‚Äãhave not been disclosed. Ishii also said that while their research vessel was conducting rare earth surveys within Japan's exclusive economic zone (EEZ) around ‚ÄçMinamitori Island from May 27 to Jun 25, a Chinese naval fleet entered ‚ÅÝthe waters on Jun 7. "We feel a strong sense of crisis that such intimidating actions ‚Äãwere taken, despite our ‚Äåactivities being limited to seabed resource surveys within our EEZ," ‚Äçhe ‚Äãsaid.

data.title: Apple to allow third-party app stores in Brazil to settle iOS case with regulator
data.description: SAO PAULO, Dec ‚Äå23 : Apple will allow other app stores besides its own in the tech giant's iOS operating system in Brazil to settle a three-year case with the country's antitrust regulator CADE, both parties said on Tuesday. An internal panel at CADE formed a majority to accept Apple's proposal of an agreement, CADE said in a statement. Apart from the app stores, the agreement also ‚Äåorders Apple to allow for third-party payment¬Ýprocessing methods for ‚Äåin-app purchases besides its own, or links to external websites for transactions.¬Ý Apple said in a statement it will make the changes to comply with CADE demands, but that these moves will open privacy and security risks to users. The California-based company said it has "worked to maintain protections against some threats," but that "these safeguards will not eliminate ‚Äçevery risk." The probe started after a complaint filed in 2022 by Uruguay-based e-commerce platform MercadoLibre over Apple's alleged restrictions on the distribution of digital goods and on in-app purchases. CADE then issued preventive measures targeting Apple in 2024, and earlier this year, its technical body ‚Äãrecommended a ruling against the ‚ÄåU.S. company, sending the case to the regulator's internal panel for a final decision. MercadoLibre, which operates across Latin America, said it acknowledged CADE's efforts "to ‚Äãface the competitive challenges" of iOS and Apple's App Store service in Brazil, but added ‚ÅÝthe agreement "only partially addresses the needs ‚Äåof more balanced rules." The agreement between CADE and Apple will last three years, ‚Äãstarting once the new terms become mandatory for app developers, the regulator said. Apple has 105 days to execute the changes it agreed ‚Äçto, the antitrust body added. The tech company is subject to a payment of up to ‚ÅÝ150 million reais ($27.09 million) in case it fully breaks the agreement, CADE said, noting Apple ‚Äãalso agreed to terminate a ‚Äåjudicial complaint it had filed against the 2024 preventive measures.¬Ý ($1 = 5.5375 ‚Äçreais) (Reporting ‚Äãby Andre RomaniEditing by Rod Nickel)

data.title: Toyota to recall over 55,000 US vehicles over loose inverter connection, NHTSA says
data.description: Dec ‚Äå23 : Toyota is recalling 55,405 vehicles in the U.S. as ‚Äåbolt inside ‚Äåthe inverter may not have been tightened properly, ‚Äçcausing incomplete contact at the inverter terminal, ‚Äãthe U.S. ‚ÄåNational Highway Traffic Safety ‚ÄãAdministration said on ‚ÅÝTuesday. The ‚Äåremedy is ‚Äãcurrently under development, NHTSA ‚Äçsaid.

data.title: Equities rise after strong US data; yen firms on currency warnings
data.description: NEW YORK/LONDON, Dec 23 : Major stock indexes were up while Treasury yields were little changed on Tuesday after stronger-than-expected U.S. economic data, while the yen shot up after warnings from Tokyo on its readiness to support the battered currency. The pan-European STOXX 600 index registered a record closing high on gains in the healthcare sector, after heavyweight Novo Nordisk secured U.S. approval of its weight-loss pill. Silver hit a record high above $70 an ounce, while gold also¬Ýtouched¬Ýa record. Data showed the U.S. economy grew faster than expected in the third quarter, driven by robust consumer spending. Early estimates showed gross domestic product increased at a 4.3 per cent annualized rate last quarter, ‚Äåfar above economists' forecast for a rise at a 3.3 per cent pace, according to a Reuters poll. "We're ‚Äåstill in this period of playing catch-up with economic data and GDP is dated... But it's showing strength in the economy, and strength that's above expectations," said Tim Ghriskey, senior portfolio strategist at Ingalls & Snyder in New York. U.S. economic releases have been delayed by a record federal government shutdown. On Wall Street at 2:49 p.m., the Dow Jones Industrial Average rose 102.88 points, or 0.21 per cent, to 48,465.56, the S&P 500 rose 28.76 points, or 0.42 per cent, to 6,907.14 and the Nasdaq Composite rose 113.51 points, or 0.48 per cent, to 23,542.33. MSCI's gauge of stocks across the globe <.MIWD00000PUS> ¬Ýrose¬Ý4.74 points, or 0.47 per cent, to ‚Äç1,020.23. Earlier, the pan-European STOXX 600 <.STOXX> index had ended up 0.34 per cent.¬Ý Novo Nordisk's U.S. listed shares were rallying more than 6 per cent on Tuesday after the U.S. Food and Drug Administration approved its weight-loss pill, giving the Danish drugmaker a competitive edge in the fast-evolving obesity treatment market. The yield on benchmark U.S. 10-year notes gave up earlier gains and was flat compared with late Monday at 4.171 per cent. The 2-year note yield, which typically moves in step with interest rate ‚Äãexpectations for the Federal Reserve, rose 3.1 basis points ‚Äåto 3.534 per cent, from 3.503 per cent late on Monday. In currencies, the yen gained against the dollar as investors weighed the odds of an imminent intervention after¬Ý Finance Minister Satsuki Katayama said Japan has a free hand in dealing with excessive moves in the yen, ‚Äãthe strongest warning so far about Tokyo's readiness to intervene in the market to arrest sharp declines in the currency. The BOJ raised rates at ‚ÅÝits December policy meeting on Friday, as expected. Against the Japanese yen , ‚Äåthe dollar weakened 0.52 per cent to 156.23. The dollar index <=USD>, which measures the greenback against a basket of currencies including the yen and the euro, ‚Äãfell 0.27 per cent to 97.97. The euro was up¬Ý0.22 per cent at $1.1786 and sterling strengthened 0.22 per cent to $1.3491. In precious metals, silver extended its record rally and hit the key $70 an ounce mark, while gold and platinum also touched historic highs. Spot silver was up 3.38 per cent ‚Äçat $71.31 while spot gold rose 1.03 per cent to $4,491.29 an ounce and spot platinum was up about 6.4 per cent at $2,255.40. In energy markets, oil prices ended Tuesday's session higher ‚ÅÝas potential sales of Venezuelan crude seized by the United States weighed and investors assessed stronger-than-expected U.S. economic data. U.S. crude settled up 0.64 per cent, or 37 cents at¬Ý$58.38 a barrel ‚Äãwhile Brent settled at $62.38 per barrel, up ‚Äå0.5 per cent, or 31 cents¬Ý on the day.

data.title: US dollar retreats as prospect of Fed rate cuts overshadows growth data
data.description: NEW YORK, Dec 23 : ‚ÄåThe U.S. dollar weakened on Tuesday in a holiday-shortened week after data showing strong growth in the world's largest economy failed to shift sentiment on a currency under pressure from expectations of Federal Reserve interest rate cuts next year. The report bolstered views that the Fed will hold off on cutting rates at its meeting in late January, with the odds currently at 87 per cent, according to LSEG estimates. U.S. rate futures now expect the U.S. central bank's next policy easing will occur in June, with two quarter-percentage-point cuts priced in for 2026. "We could see a lower dollar next year at least in the first quarter because the Fed is going to be increasingly forced to admit that the labor market is not in a good place," said Erik Bregar, director of FX and precious metals risk management at Silver Gold Bull in Toronto. "The ‚ÄåFed may be forced to cave a little bit (on rate cuts), even more than they have so far. The market wants ‚Äåcuts. And the odds are that we're going to get a dovish Fed chair that will try to make that happen." U.S. gross domestic product rose at a 4.3 per cent annualized rate in the last quarter, the first estimate from the Commerce Department's Bureau of Economic Analysis showed. Economists polled by Reuters had forecast GDP would rise at a 3.3 per cent pace in the third quarter.¬Ý The dollar trimmed its losses against the yen and euro following the release of the GDP report. "A surface-level read on this (GDP) data gives the impression that the economy is roaring into an acceleration following a very short stumble around the anticipation of tariff announcements," Tom Simons, chief U.S. economist at Jefferies, said in a research note. "However, it is difficult to see how there won't be substantial downward revisions ‚Äçin the final cut, or dramatic payback in Q4." Following the GDP report, the dollar cut its losses against the yen and last traded at 156.26 yen, still down 0.5 per cent. The euro also pared gains against the dollar after the data and was last at $1.1779, still up 0.2 per cent on the day. JAPAN INTERVENTION WATCH The yen gained against the dollar in earlier trading after the strongest indication yet from authorities of Tokyo's readiness to intervene. The Japanese currency hovered near lows against its major peers in recent sessions, with the threat of intervention keeping yen bears at bay. But near-term yen weakness is likely to persist, ‚Äãanalysts say, as the cautious tone from the Bank of Japan last ‚Äåweek hinted at a slow pace of rate hikes next year. Japanese Finance Minister Satsuki Katayama said on Tuesday that Japan has a free hand in dealing with excessive moves in the yen. "I would call it an un-hawkish rate hike last week from the Bank of Japan. It feels like every time we go into a ‚ÄãBOJ meeting, the market gets too excited that we're going to have the start of a rate-hiking cycle, but the officials always tamp down those expectations," said Silver Gold Bull's Bregar. "So a lot of ‚ÅÝthe price action is really just people getting out of those bets on the ‚Äåyen going up, after they just keep getting disappointed." Elsewhere in currency markets, the dollar index, which measures the U.S. currency against six rivals, slipped 0.2 per cent to 98.02, extending its losses into ‚Äãa second straight day. The index fell to its lowest level since early October and was on course for a 1.4 per cent decrease for the month, which would be its biggest fall since August, and a 9.6 per cent drop for the year, which would be its steepest annual decline since 2017. Strategists at MUFG said the ‚Äçdollar's drop this year is unlikely to be a one-off deal. The dollar also dipped after data showed U.S. consumer confidence worsened in December. The Conference Board said on Tuesday its consumer confidence index ‚ÅÝfell 3.8 points to 89.1 this month. Economists polled by Reuters had forecast the index would be at 91.0. In other currencies, sterling rose 0.2 per cent against the dollar to $1.3483, after earlier hitting a 12-week peak of $1.35. Against the Swiss franc, the ‚Äãgreenback fell 0.4 per cent to 0.7886 francs. Earlier in ‚Äåthe session, the dollar dropped to a three-month low of 0.7867 francs. Currency ¬Ý ¬Ý ¬Ý ¬Ý ¬Ý ¬Ý ¬Ý bid prices at 23 December ‚Äã 07:28 p.m. GMT Descript RIC Last U.S. Pct YTD High Low ion Close Chang Pct Bid Bid Previous e Session Dollar 97.96 98.237 -0.27 -9.71 per cent 98.237 97. index per cent 85 Euro/Dol 1.1787 1.1759 0.24 per cent 13.85 per cent $1.180 $1. lar 2 176 2 Dollar/Y 156.22 156.85 -0.37 -0.69 per cent 156.84 155 en per cent 5 .7 Euro/Yen 184.09‚Äã 184.68 -0.32 12.79 per cent 184.68 183 per cent .44 Dollar/S 0.788 0.7918 -0.45 -13.15 0.7915 0.7 wiss per cent per cent 867 Sterling 1.3492 1.346 0.25 per cent 7.88 per cent $1.351 $1. /Dollar 8 346 6‚Äã Dollar/C 1.3691 1.3747 -0.4 per cent -4.78 per cent 1.3754 1.3 anadian 69 Aussie/D 0.6696 0.6657 0.62 per cent 8.25 per cent $0.670 $0. ollar 1 665 7 Euro/Swi 0.9287 0.931 -0.25 -1.14 per cent 0.9315 0.9 ss per cent 284 Euro/Ste 0.8733 0.8735 -0.02 5.56 per cent 0.8739 0.8 rling per cent 721 NZ 0.5836 0.5794 0.73 per cent 4.3 per cent $0.584 0.5 Dollar/D 3 795 ollar Dollar/N 10.0279 10.091 -0.63 -11.77 10.113 10. orway ‚Äã per cent per cent 4 03 Euro/Nor 11.8192 11.8764 -0.48 0.44 per cent 11.9 11. way per cent 82 Dollar/S 9.1705 9.2332 -0.68 -16.76 9.2409 9.1 weden per cent per cent 698 Euro/Swe 10.8088 10.8594 -0.47 -5.74 per cent 10.867 10. den per cent 5 807 8

data.title: US judge blocks Texas app store age law meant to protect children
data.description: WASHINGTON, ‚ÄåDec 23 : A federal judge in Texas on Tuesday blocked Texas from enforcing a new state law aimed at protecting children by requiring app stores and developers to verify the age of users, in a win for Apple , Alphabet's Google and other technology companies. U.S. District Judge Robert Pitman in Austin issued a preliminary injunction against the state‚Äôs App Store Accountability Act, finding the ‚Äåmeasure likely violates the U.S. Constitution's speech protections under the ‚ÄåFirst Amendment. The law, which was set to take effect in January, would require parental consent to download apps or make in-app purchases for users aged below 18. Pitman‚Äôs ruling was a win for the Computer & Communications Industry Association, which sued to stop the law. The order was a setback to Texas and its efforts to regulate smartphone use by ‚Äçchildren and teenagers, part of a broader crackdown by some U.S. states to combat potentially harmful effects of social media on young people. Australia this month became the first country to ban social media for children under 16. The Texas attorney general‚Äôs office did not immediately ‚Äãrespond to a request for comment. Pitman ‚Äåacknowledged concerns about children‚Äôs online safety but said ‚Äúhowever compelling the policy concerns, and however widespread the agreement that the issue must be addressed, the court ‚Äãremains bound by the rule of law.‚Äù The Washington, D.C.-based Computer & Communications Industry Association welcomed the ‚ÅÝorder. The order will "preserve the First Amendment rights ‚Äåof app stores, app developers, parents, and younger internet users,‚Äù said Stephanie Joyce, who ‚Äãheads the group‚Äôs litigation center. ‚ÄúIt also protects parents‚Äô inviolate right to use their own judgment in safeguarding their children online using the myriad tools ‚Äçour members provide.‚Äù Google and Apple, which were not plaintiffs, did not immediately respond to requests ‚ÅÝfor comment. The Computer & Communications Industry Association said the law would have placed ‚Äúburdens on app stores, developers, minors, ‚Äãand parents that are ‚Äåcompletely disproportionate to any harm policymakers were attempting to remedy.‚Äù

data.title: Trump's AI hiring campaign draws interest from 25,000 hopefuls
data.description: WASHINGTON, Dec 23 : Roughly 25,000 people have expressed interest in joining the Trump administration's cadre of engineers known as Tech Force, a senior Trump administration official said on Tuesday, as the U.S. government looks to install staff with artificial ‚Äåintelligence expertise in federal roles. The Trump ‚Äåadministration will use that list to recruit software and data engineers, in addition to other tech roles, said Scott Kupor, director of the U.S. Office of Personnel Management, in a post on X. Reuters could ‚Äçnot independently verify the 25,000 figure.¬Ý The interested candidates will compete for 1,000 spots in the first Tech Force cohort. The recruits will spend two years working on technology ‚Äãprojects inside federal agencies, ‚Äåincluding the Departments of Homeland Security, Veterans Affairs, and Justice, among other government offices, Kupor ‚Äãsaid previously.¬Ý The hiring initiative is part of the Trump administration's ‚ÅÝAI agenda. Previous U.S. ‚Äåpresidents have launched similar initiatives to bring tech ‚Äãtalent into government, including former President Joe Biden. President Donald Trump, in the first months of his ‚Äçsecond term, focused on eliminating government jobs, with exceptions ‚ÅÝfor positions his administration said were necessary to maintain "national security." ‚ÄãTech Force is ‚Äåa departure from the downsizing campaign.¬Ý

data.title: US dollar falters as markets focus on Fed easing over growth data
data.description: NEW ‚ÄåYORK/LONDON, Dec 23 : The U.S. dollar fell on Tuesday in a holiday-shortened week, weighed down by expectations of more rate cuts next year, though it trimmed losses against the yen and euro after data showed strong third-quarter growth in the world's largest economy. The report bolstered views that the Federal Reserve will hold off on cutting rates at its January meeting, with the odds currently at 87 per cent, according to LSEG estimates. U.S. rate futures now expect the next Fed easing to occur in June, with two rate cuts of 25 basis points each priced in for 2026. "We could see a lower dollar, at least in the first quarter next year, because the Fed is going to be increasingly forced to admit that the labor market ‚Äåis not in a good place," said Erik Bregar, director, FX & precious metals risk management at Silver Gold Bull ‚Äåin Toronto. "You have these inflation hawks on the committee that are keeping U.S. rates on the long end a little bit higher than they should be," he added. Tuesday's data showed that U.S. gross domestic product rose at a 4.3 per cent annualized rate in the last quarter, the first estimate from the Commerce Department's Bureau of Economic Analysis showed. Economists polled by Reuters had forecast GDP would rise at a 3.3 per cent pace in the third quarter. Brian Jacobsen, chief economic strategist at Annex Wealth Management in Brookfield, Wisconsin, however, said the strong GDP number was misleading. "Health care spending drove the GDP gains, which isn't necessarily healthy. It is consistent with where employment gains has been, though," Jacobsen ‚Äçwrote in emailed comments after the data was released. "Businesses are eating through their inventories that they stocked up on earlier in the year so we should see a topping-off of inventories going into 2026." Following the report, the dollar cut its losses against the yen to 156.39 yen, still down 0.4 per cent. JAPAN INTERVENTION WATCH The yen earlier gained against the dollar after the strongest indication yet from authorities of Tokyo's readiness to intervene.¬Ý The Japanese unit hovered near recent lows against major peers, with the threat of intervention keeping yen bears at bay ‚Äãfor now. But near-term yen weakness is likely to persist, analysts ‚Äåsay, as the cautious tone from the Bank of Japan last week hinted at a slow pace of rate hikes next year. Finance Minister Satsuki Katayama said Japan has a free hand in dealing with excessive moves in the yen. "I would call it an un-hawkish rate hike last week ‚Äãfrom the Bank of Japan. It feels like every time we go into a BOJ meeting, the market gets too excited that we're going to have the start of a ‚ÅÝrate-hiking cycle but the officials always tamp down those expectations," said Silver Gold ‚ÄåBull's Bregar. "So a lot of the price action is really just people getting out of those bets on the yen going up, after they just keep getting ‚Äãdisappointed." The euro also slid, paring gains against the dollar to $1.1786, still up 0.2 per cent on the day. The dollar index, which measures the U.S. currency against six rivals, slipped 0.2 per cent to 98.07, extending losses into a second day. The index is at its lowest level since early October and ‚Äçon course for a 1.4 per cent decline for the month, its biggest since August, and a 9.6 per cent drop for the year, its steepest annual fall since 2017. Strategists at MUFG said ‚ÅÝthe drop for the dollar this year is unlikely to be a one-off with scope for further gains ahead. The dollar also dipped after U.S. consumer confidence worsened in December. The Conference Board said on ‚ÄãTuesday its consumer confidence index fell 3.8 ‚Äåpoints to 89.1 this month. Economists polled by Reuters had forecast the index at 91.0.

data.title: ServiceNow to buy Armis for $7.75 billion as AI-fueled cyber risks surge
data.description: Dec 23 : ServiceNow on Tuesday agreed to buy cybersecurity startup Armis for $7.75 billion, in the enterprise software maker's biggest-ever deal, as it bolsters its services to help counter rising cyberattacks driven by AI adoption. Through the deal, the company aims to integrate Alphabet-backed Armis' security features such as device scanning and threat detection, a ‚Äåcrucial advantage amid sophisticated cyberattacks that have hit ‚Äåcompanies ranging from Microsoft to UnitedHealth Group. However, ServiceNow's shares fell about 3 per cent, as investors worried over the company's recent splurge on acquisitions. "Our security stack, with the acquisition of Armis, is very well positioned, so we won't need to do any more M&A in security space," ServiceNow Chief Financial Officer Gina Mastantuono ‚Äçtold Reuters. The company expects the deal, scheduled to close in the second half of 2026, to triple the market opportunity for its security and risk business. "ServiceNow's buying spree shows it's trying to get ahead of competitors on the orchestration ‚Äãand governance front by acquiring ‚ÄåIP, tech leadership, and customer bases," said Rebecca Wettemann, CEO of Valoir, an industry research and advisory firm. In recent months, ServiceNow ‚Äãhas bought security firm Veza, AI company Moveworks and sales automation platform Logik.ai, formerly ‚ÅÝLogik.io. The company closed its $2.85 billion acquisition ‚Äåof Moveworks this month. Armis, valued at $6.1 ‚ÄØbillion in a funding round in November, ‚Äãwas preparing for an initial public offering. The startup's latest funding was led by the alternative investment platform of Goldman Sachs. Existing ‚Äçinvestor CapitalG, Alphabet's VC arm, also participated in the round. Armis, founded in 2015, ‚ÅÝunveiled a three-year plan last month to reach $1 billion in annual recurring revenue after ‚Äãcrossing the $300 million milestone in ‚ÄåAugust.

data.title: Amazon's Zoox to recall 332 US vehicles over software error
data.description: WASHINGTON, ‚ÄåDec 23 : Amazon's self-driving unit Zoox said on Tuesday it is recalling 332 self-driving robotaxis because vehicles may cross the yellow center line and drive into or stop in front of oncoming traffic near intersections, the National Highway Traffic Safety Administration said. Zoox said it has addressed the issue through a software update and said the issue did not result in any collisions. The ‚Äåcompany opened an investigation after¬Ýa Zoox robotaxi made a ‚Äåwide right turn in late August, crossed partially into the opposing travel lane, and temporarily stopped in front of the oncoming travel lane. Zoox identified 62 instances where a robotaxi crossed the lane line, partially or fully, unnecessarily. "We have proactively identified some instances where our vehicles were making maneuvers that, while common for human drivers, didn't meet ‚Äçour standards," Zoox said. Zoox said it engaged in ongoing conversations with NHTSA about the issue. In May, Zoox recalled 270 vehicles and issued a software update to improve how its vehicles track nearby pedestrians and prevent movement when someone is close after an unoccupied robotaxi ‚Äãwas involved in an April ‚Äå8 crash with a passenger car in Las Vegas. In April, the National Highway Traffic Safety Administration¬Ýclosed a probe into 258 Zoox vehicles¬Ýover a braking issue ‚Äãafter Zoox issued a recall to update their software to address an issue that could ‚ÅÝcause unexpected hard braking. NHTSA in August ‚Äåcertified Zoox vehicles for demonstration use and closed a probe, which the U.S. auto ‚Äãsafety regulator began in 2022, into whether they had complied with federal requirements. Other automakers have issued recalls over software issues in self-driving vehicles.¬Ý Alphabet ‚Äçunit Waymo earlier this month recalled its¬Ýself-driving vehicles after Texas officials said they illegally passed ‚ÅÝschool buses at least 19 times since the start of the school year. NHTSA opened a ‚Äãprobe¬Ýin October into Waymo vehicles¬Ýnear ‚Äåschool buses.

data.title: US dollar trims losses after stronger-than-expected growth data
data.description: NEW ‚ÄåYORK, Dec 23 : The U.S. dollar curbed its losses against the yen and euro on Tuesday, after data showed the world's largest economy posted higher-than-expected ‚Äågrowth in the third ‚Äåquarter, reinforcing expectations the Federal Reserve will pause cutting rates at its January meeting. Data showed that U.S. gross domestic product rose at ‚Äça 4.3 per cent annualized rate in the last quarter, the first estimate from the Commerce Department's Bureau of Economic ‚ÄãAnalysis showed. ‚ÄåEconomists polled by Reuters had forecast GDP would rise at ‚Äãa 3.3 per cent pace in the third ‚ÅÝquarter. Following the report, ‚Äåthe dollar cut its losses ‚Äãagainst the yen to 156.11 yen, still down 0.6 per cent. The ‚Äçeuro also slid, paring gains against ‚ÅÝthe dollar to $1.1786, still up 0.2 per cent ‚Äãon the day. (Reporting ‚Äåby Gertrude Chavez-Dreyfuss; ‚ÄçEditing ‚Äãby Chizu Nomiyama )
